title: Wollaston Manchester Memorial
crew_summary: 7 killed (friendly fire incident - shot down by RAF Beaufighter)
narrative: This is one of the most tragic cases in the East Midlands — a friendly fire incident. On the night of 21-22 June 1941, seven Avro Manchester bombers of 207 Squadron took off from RAF Waddington to bomb the docks at Boulogne. L7314, piloted by 21-year-old Flying Officer Withers (a contemporary of the legendary Guy Gibson at 83 Squadron, Scampton), was returning at about 6,000 feet when it was attacked by a Beaufighter night fighter crewed by Pilot Officers Smith and Lusty, who had been scrambled to intercept reported enemy raiders. The Manchester was set ablaze and crashed near Poplars Farm, Wollaston. Some crew members attempted to escape the burning wreck, but part of the bomb load exploded before they could get clear. Nobody survived. Traces of only five bodies were recovered. The Beaufighter crew were subsequently court-martialled, and blame was also apportioned to the ground control station that had vectored them. The Avro Manchester itself was notoriously unreliable — only 200 were built before the type was replaced by the four-engined Lancaster — making its crews doubly vulnerable. F/O Withers held the DFC; Fl/Sgt Brown the DFM — both decorated men killed by their own side.

crew.name: John Douglas George Withers DFC
crew.fate_detail: Killed when the Manchester was shot down by a RAF Beaufighter night fighter in a tragic friendly fire incident. Part of the bomb load exploded before crew could escape the burning wreck.
crew.bio: Aged 21, holder of the DFC. A contemporary of the legendary Guy Gibson at nearby 83 Squadron, Scampton. Killed by friendly fire.

crew.name: Alick Michael James
crew.fate_detail: Killed in the friendly fire shootdown and subsequent explosion.
crew.bio: Second pilot serving with the RAF.

crew.name: William Brown DFM
crew.fate_detail: Killed in the friendly fire shootdown. A decorated airman killed by his own side.
crew.bio: Flight Sergeant and observer, holder of the DFM. One of two decorated airmen in the crew, both killed by friendly fire.

crew.name: Michael Vincent Browne
crew.fate_detail: Killed in the friendly fire shootdown.
crew.bio: Flight Sergeant serving as wireless operator.

crew.name: Allan Malone
crew.fate_detail: Killed in the friendly fire shootdown.
crew.bio: Flight Sergeant serving as wireless operator.

crew.name: Stanley Veitch
crew.fate_detail: Killed in the friendly fire shootdown.

crew.name: John Allan Maville
crew.fate_detail: Killed in the friendly fire shootdown. The only Canadian in the crew.
crew.bio: Canadian air gunner serving with the RCAF. The only non-British crew member.

cause_of_loss: Friendly fire — shot down by RAF Beaufighter night fighter
operation_target: Returning from bombing raid on docks at Boulogne, France
memorial_details: On 22 June 2021 — eighty years to the day — a memorial was dedicated near Poplars Farm, Wollaston, close to the crash site. The unveiling was attended by relatives of five crew members and by representatives of today's No. 207 Squadron and RAF Association. The Hatton Rotary Club (Wellingborough) led the memorial campaign. Publicly accessible near the crash site.

external_narratives.summary: Official RAF news article about the unveiling of the Wollaston memorial on 22 June 2021, the 80th anniversary of the crash. The memorial commemorates the seven crew of Manchester L7314 shot down by a Beaufighter night fighter in a tragic friendly fire incident while returning from bombing Boulogne.
key_details: Attended by relatives of five crew members
Official RAF acknowledgment of the friendly fire incident

external_narratives.summary: Forces News coverage of the memorial unveiling at Wollaston, reporting on the tragic circumstances of the Manchester bomber being shot down by friendly forces and the 80-year campaign to commemorate the crew.
key_details: National military news coverage of the memorial dedication

external_narratives.summary: Local newspaper article detailing the friendly fire circumstances. German aircraft were active over Lincolnshire and one followed a course overlapping L7314's track. No. 10 Group Fighter Command scrambled Beaufighters from 25 Squadron at RAF Wittering, and the ground controller mistakenly vectored them onto the Manchester.
key_details: Ground controller insisted target was hostile and ordered shoot-down
Beaufighter pilot was 'in two minds' but fired cannon and machine guns
Beaufighter crew (P/O Smith and P/O Lusty) were court-martialled
Blame also apportioned to the ground control station
Only traces of five bodies recovered from crash

external_narratives.summary: Background article about the Avro Manchester aircraft type and its role as the predecessor to the Lancaster. Only 200 Manchesters were built before being replaced by the four-engined Lancaster, making their crews particularly vulnerable.
key_details: Only 200 Manchesters built before type replaced by Lancaster
Provides context on the notoriously unreliable aircraft

external_narratives.summary: Hatton Rotary Club page documenting their campaign to create the memorial for Manchester L7314 at Wollaston. The Rotary Club led the memorial project and coordinated with crew relatives and the RAF.
key_details: Hatton Rotary Club (Wellingborough) led the memorial campaign
Coordinated with crew relatives and RAF Association

external_narratives.summary: BAAA formal accident record for the Manchester I crash at Wollaston on 22 June 1941, recording seven fatalities. Catalogued under aircraft type Avro 679 Manchester I.

local_contacts.role: Local authority responsible for memorial upkeep
